speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- you were arrested. Who arrested you, the Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarian plainclothes detectives. They processed me at the police station in Budapest after they interrogated me. And some very physical interrogation, beating up and so on and so on, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of questions were they asking?

speaker: interviewee
text: They tried-- they didn't tell me what it is. Although I knew-- I had a proclivity of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what's happening. [BACKGROUND NOISE] They asked-- there were anti-Jewish laws at that time that, you know, you could not have any physical, intimate connection with non-Jews, which was also under heavy, heavy prison threats.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they tried to tell me that they know that I had some intimate connection with non-Jews. And-- and-- but they-- they tried to divert me from what it is. But they finally said, what did I-- what kind of documents did I-- I mean, I didn't do nothing. I-- I-- I was innocent in the whole thing. I wasn't involved in that.

speaker: interviewee
text: They finally brought in the gendarmes, you know, the ones with the feathers-- feather hats. And they [? chained ?] me, shackled me, and they took me down to the train station. And we were traveling all day, until we came-- until about 11:00 in the night. And the train stopped, and we got off the train. It was very, very dark, and in the distance, I saw a building. And they took me into that building, and it was about 11:00, 11:30 at night. And there the interrogations started all over again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you been fed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The gendarmes were standing by either side. And there was an interrogator, a civilian man who was talking very crudely, and yelling and shouting, and slapping me from time to time that I'm not telling the truth. And asking me what this piece-- this piece of paper is, that piece of paper is that they took from my apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why do I study English? I must be an English spy or something like that. They try to divert you. They finally-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they kept me till about 4:00 in the morning. And they finally threw me into a cell where there was-- I was really very faint because there was groaning and moaning. You know, and I-- people were talking Yiddish. I says, what are you doing here? I spoke to them in Yiddish. And they told me that they are originally from Slovakia, and that they were arrested and they're being held there and they are being interrogated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Lo and behold-- well, it's a very long story. That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to go in too much. I-- my other sister had-- another one that was involved in the papers was married to-- to a fellow that originated from Kishinev, from Bessarabia. And when the Hungarians took over Transylvania, and the Russians occupied Bessarabia, he had a choice. He could have gone to Romania and stay as a Romanian citizen, but he didn't. Because he didn't, he was neither a Romanian, nor a Hungarian, and nor a Russian. So the Hungarians said, you are nobody, and they deported him. And that was in 1942 also, at the beginning of 1942. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so after about three months that they were deported to Galicia, they came back. They walked back all the way to Satu Mare.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister and her husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her husband. They came back. And of course, they took false documents. And they moved to Cluj, which is a city in-- in Romanian. Well, it was a city in Romania, which was also Hungary at that time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: As I was throwing into the cell, about a half an hour later, a shadow came in. And as he opened his mouth, in Jewish, he said, where am I? I recognized his voice. That's my brother-in-law that was also brought in. So they arrested everybody, and-- along with all the Slovak Jews, and-- and I heard what was going on. The following day, I was taken in again for interrogation. And I really-- I was really innocent. I had nothing to do with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they thought that I was connected because they also found some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] postcards that I sent to my sister in Cluj, and they searched her house. So they thought for sure that I'm also involved. They kept me there for eight days, and-- and-- prisoner. And I was able to talk my way out. And after--

speaker: interviewer
text: To talk your way out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And after eight days, they-- they let me go. So before I left, all the Jews that were arrested there asked me to go into Kosice, which is Kaschau. It's a city in-- in Czechoslovakia-- Slovakia now. And to tell the Jewish community what's going on there, because it was hermetically isolated. Nobody knew what was going on there, although the Jewish community from Kosice said, let's send in food. But they could only send it so by a third party. Like, the Jews could not go in there. So as they released me, I went to Kosice, and I went to the community to tell them what's happening to the Jews. And-- and that-- it-- it was called Hidosnemeti, [BACKGROUND NOISE] H-I-D-O-S-N-E-M-E-T-I, Hidosnemeti. And that was-- well, that was a small, little armory that they specially kept for anti-espionage. Anything-- anybody that was charged with that type of thing, they took them there. And I told the Jewish-- I had opportunity to talk to the Jewish community, telling them how many that are arrested there, and what's happening to them, how they're being beaten up. Some people were beaten up horribly, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you received in the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [INAUDIBLE] The whole-- the whole board met. It was Sukkot, I remember. And we met in a sukkah. And everybody came to hear that I should tell them what's happening in there. I conveyed the message to 'em. And the following day, I was able to take a train and to go back to Budapest. And when I came back to Budapest, it was when my boss saw me, he thought that I came back from-- from yonder. Anyhow, so to pick up, 1944, March, when the Germans came in--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the only time you were bothered while you were in Budapest, the only time you were arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. But then you had to put on the yellow star in 1944. And you couldn't go on the train. You couldn't go here. You couldn't go there. There were some very strict anti-Jewish restrictions that came out, anti-Jewish laws. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then at the end of March, I received a summons, conscription-like, that I have to report for-- it was called the work-- to a work brigade that was actually under the jurisdiction of the military. Like--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Hungarian--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarian military. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Like, all we got was a cap-- a military cap-- and we had to wear a yellow arm band.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the Jewish star?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, just a yellow armband. We were under the same rules and regulation as the Army. We were fed by the Army. Except-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] except we did all the dirty work-- digging ditches, cleaning up after air raids, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: We? Who was we?

speaker: interviewee
text: The brigade. The brigade, the people that were conscripted into this type of brigade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it all Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish, all Jewish. There were also-- there were some other brigades that were non-Jews, like Serbs, you know, and some other minority. But we had nothing to do with them. But this was strictly Jewish. All Jewish kids had to go into that brigade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there an age span?

speaker: interviewee
text: Age span, yes. Now for instance, the-- the one 19, 20, 21, and 22 had-- were conscripted prior to us. Now, I was conscripted for April 22. And 23 and 24 was conscripted at the same time as me. And then, later on, 25 and 26 was also conscripted. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had no contact at all with my parents. [BACKGROUND NOISE] At all. I did not know what-- what was going on in Satu Mare or what happened. And I had to report to a remote place that was on the Slovakian border. It was called [? Yolsva ?], Y-O-L-S-V-A, [? Yolsva ?]. It's pronounced 'Yolshva.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But prior to my-- to my being conscripted there, whilst I couldn't travel on the train, but I could send a postcard. And I sent a postcard to my parents in Satu Mare that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] I'm being conscripted. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And I don't know when-- when I'll see-- see them. And I told them where I'm going. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Lo and behold, in the end of April-- April-- I remember the date. April 29th, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I got a postcard from my father. And at the time-- at that time, he told me that they were herded into a ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he told me the approximate place where he is. And he says, I don't know where your mother is. They were separated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He didn't tell me but I found out later that my other sister, the one that was originally arrested because of the false documentation, was eventually released, And she went into hiding. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The only thing, he wrote me this letter, and he told me that they don't live anymore at home. They are all in a ghetto. And he only wrote me Yiddish. He said, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. And I don't know whether you understand [? Yiddish ?]. He says, 'Your brother, Israel'-- which [INAUDIBLE] you might remember-- 'needs great healing.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was the last time I heard. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] My other sister, that I told you that was in Cluj, that was arrested, she was eventually put in a-- in-- into a camp for aliens. And in-- in 1944, that was in May-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] towards the end of May, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the way I know it, they were-- they were deported to Auschwitz. The entire city of Satu Mare was deported. We didn't know at that time. We didn't know where they were deported. The rumor was that they were resettled. I mean, that was the official-- the official version, that they were resettled. And they are apparently, they are being given some very nice place. But we later, of course, found out that they were deported to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As for myself, I was in the work brigade, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to the camp that you were supposed to get to if you couldn't go on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had the document as a-- conscript. As a conscript, I-- I was under military rule.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could go on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was under military rule, and all I had to do is to show that-- that summons. And that was-- that was official. And that, of course, applied to everybody that was conscripted. They-- on this document, they could only go to that particular place. They couldn't go to any other place, but only to report to-- to whatever place we were conscripted to. We were-- OK, from [? Yolsva ?], we were transferred to Miskolc. That's a city in Hungary-- M-I-S-K-O-L-C. And there we were assigned to a military camp of artillery. And we were in charge-- we were in charge of cleaning up their mechanical things, and to dig ditches and fortifications, and you name it, that type of thing. We were about 240 to 250 in this particular brigade. And we were known as such. We had a number. A hundred. Number 107/23. That was our number. You belonged to that brigade. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And life was tolerable there. We-- we worked, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you fed?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were fed by the military, yes. No shortage, as far as that goes. But we arrive into Miskolc exactly when they were rounding-- rounding up the ghetto and putting them into the wagons, to the freight-- to the freight cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Miskolc. The ghetto at Miskolc. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, half of our brigade belonged-- half our brigade consisted of young men from Miskolc, that their hometown was Miskolc. And when we arrived in Miskolc and they heard that they are being deported, that the Germans are-- and the Hungarians, they did it in conjunction-- were rounding up the Jews and they're taking them away in freight trains, five of our comrades ventured out. They went AWOL. And they went to see their parents, you know, to say goodbye to them. They were caught, and they were also thrown into the-- into the freight cars, and we never saw them again. And that was then. But again, we couldn't conceive the idea that something very tragic is happening. The-- the rumor was, again, they're resettling, and-- and they are going to be put into a nice place, and so on. As a matter of fact, you know, there are even some-- certain postcards came back with picturesque-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] picturesque postcards saying that they are in a very nice place and that they are in good order, and nothing is happening. Which was, of course, wasn't true. I mean, they were forced to write these cards. We were in Miskolc until about [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] middle to end of August.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any special job when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was lucky because I became the chief-- the commander-- commanding officer's boy. And I was his helper, and I was with him. I-- I cooked for him and I cleaned his laundry and shined his boots. And so I was an officer's boy. So I didn't have to go out to work. I was just constantly with him. Wherever he went, I-- I went with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was-- I had it fairly, fairly good. In 19-- like I said, towards the end of August, we were transferred to Budapest. And we were put into a-- into a factory that was producing cases for anti-aircraft shells. Now, that was the duty of the-- of the brigade.

speaker: interviewee
text: But by that time, Budapest was bombed constantly. It was bombed constantly. And we endured some horrific, horrific bombardment. In that area where we were, that was a factory area, a highly industrialized area. And that was being targeted right along. And I remember one Sunday morning, we were so heavily bombarded. And being in a factory that made those cases, there was a lot of lumber. And so the lumber was piled up real high in the whole-- in the backyard. And for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shelter, for air-raid shelters, they dug trenches underneath those piles of lumber. And whenever there was a-- the sirens were go on, we would have to run into those trenches to hide. And that particular Sunday morning-- and I believe it was very close to Rosh Hashanah, or maybe the day before Rosh Hashanah-- we received a bombardment from 8:30 in the morning till 2:30 in the afternoon. And at one point, there was one bomb that fell about 20 meters from us and it created a tremendous crater. We thought that we were all dead, but luckily, we were safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were Allied bombs that were falling?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Allied bombs, yes. At that time, the bombing was so heavy. Budapest got it really strong because the Americans would-- would bomb during the day, and the British would bomb during the night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was OK. This was the way life was going on until September the 15th. And on September the 15, the Hungarian government tried to capitulate. And on a Sunday, the vice-- the regent, Horty-- H-O-R-T-Y-- made an announcement that the Hungarians are suing for peace, or to surrender. Well, by that time, of course, they were occupied by the Germans. And in no time, the Germans swooped down all over. They arrested the regent and everybody. And the Arrow Cross people took over the government. And all hell broke loose at that time. Because on Monday morning, I ventured out because my commanding officer gave me a false document saying-- which was in the form of a book, which every military personnel had to have. It-- with a picture on it, with everything. It said that I'm Greek Catholic. So I ventured out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the same officer you had always worked for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I ventured out with-- from our compound, and I went to see my boss for whom I worked. He had a [INAUDIBLE], a daughter, an only daughter. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I tried to sort of smuggle out the daughter. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: You tried to smuggle out the daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: His daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: His daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to their apartment and thought that I can take her to a safe place. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I had to-- I had to make my way in from the compound into the city. And as I was going in, there were corpses already lying on the streets, covered with newspapers. These Arrow Cross people were going around killing Jews. Because the minute the regent made that announcement by the Hungarians, the Jewish people ventured out of the Jewish-designated houses, which were formed into a ghetto. And from then on, there was open, blatant physical [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] persecution and killings going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the Arrow Cross people?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Arrow Cross. They rounded up-- wherever they could find a Jew, they rounded them up. And in the meantime, for some reason or another, there is-- there are several bridges over the Danube. One of the bridge, the Saint Margaret Bridge, was bombed and it was destroyed. But they put up a temporary bridge on pontoons. And they rounded up the Jews every day, and they-- these Arrow Cross. And they would take them to these pontoons at night, shoot them, and-- into the Danube-- kill them. As for myself, my commanding officer, about six to eight days later, after the Arrow Cross took over, they arrested him as being a friend of the Jews. And I was left alone in the apartment, because we had requisitioned an apartment for him, being as a commanding officer. And also, the office of the brigade was there. And I was left alone there, and I didn't know what to do, where to go. And when they arrested him, they burst in-- the Germans and the Arrow Cross-- with machine guns. And I-- we were on the second floor. And I heard them coming, so I sort of dived under a couch in one of the bedrooms. And I was hiding there. And they came in, and whoever they found there in-- in that apartment, including the commanding officer, they took away. And I was left alone there with one other fellow that was-- that was also hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop right here.
